question: A Select form field uses a FEEL expression that returns the shorthand `[{"value": "fr"}, {"value": "de"}]` with no `label` property on any option. Which value types does the form accept in this shorthand?

options.text: Any non-null type, including objects and lists.
Only `string` values, since the value also serves as the label.
Numeric types only, because labels must be strings and FEEL coerces values to numbers.

a.text: The supported-types section states that when no `label` is provided, the `value` parameter is restricted to `string`, `number`, and `boolean`. The label-less shorthand falls into this case.

b.text: Any non-null type is allowed only when a `label` is provided. The shorthand omits the label, so this broader rule does not apply.

c.text: Even without a label, the docs allow `number` and `boolean` values in addition to `string`; the shorthand is not restricted to strings only.

d.text: There is no rule that FEEL coerces option values to numbers; the docs explicitly include `string` and `boolean` alongside `number` for the no-label case.

explanation: The supported-types section of the options page distinguishes two cases. With a `label`, the `value` can be any non-null type. Without a `label` (`[{value: ...}]` or `["fr", ...]`), `value` is restricted to `string`, `number`, or `boolean` because the value itself is then also used as the display label.
